content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. A Movie changed your point view of life

**Score:** 80
**Body:** Is there like a movie you kept thinking of a lot in your life? Like a movie/ made you see a different diff perspective  :) ? Im gen curious 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Big Short was one that made me realize how fucked the system is.


Spotlight was one that made me realize how many people will turn a blind eye to or even protect the worst people due to tribalism.
2. Matrix
3. Yes Man, got out a lot more after watching that one. Didn’t say yes to everything, just made more of an effort to find things to do.
4. Perfect days 
5. Not a movie but The Wire changed my views on our society. The way it portrays our society and how it functions or dysfunctions and how its systemic. It makes you wonder if our problems can ever be solved.
6. I watched Threads in college. Before this movie I believed war to be almost romantic in a way. Where people would band together to save the day and in the end the good guys would always win. Action hero stuff.

Threads literally changed something in my brain and I never saw war, or the world, in the same way again. 

  
Part of me sometimes wishes I had never seen it.

Even though it brought a despair I can't describe, this movie also made me hyper aware of how fragile and precious every single life is.
7. Gattaca 
8. About Time
9. Children of men.  Left a profound impression on me.  It’s scary how that movie is coming true in a lot of ways.  Not the infertility but the general collapse of society and treatment of immigrants. 
10. Flow

## 2. Any movies on deteriorating mental health?

**Score:** 52
**Body:** I have watched Melancholia, Aftersun, Little Ms. Sunshine, Anomalisa and few others. Movies which really capture the meaninglessness of modern society? Can we make a list?

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Fight Club 
2. Shining
3. Harold and Maude (1971) - kind of, but the opposite? It's about a disillusioned and suicidal young man that befriends an older woman and they explore the meaning of life, all with an overall nihilistic view on things.
  - I have a very special place in my heart for this film. I don't know if it is still happening, but in Essen (Germany) there was an old theatre that showed it weekly and everything was as if it were the seventies. 
4. Girl, interrupted
5. Shutter Island - a masterpiece on psychology!
6. Falling Down, I’m Thinking of Ending Things, Synecdoche New York, Oslo August 31, If I Had Legs I’d Kick You, The Father
7. Requiem 
8. Bug is probably the most clinically accurate for delusional parasitosis.  Others include Clean Shaven, The Shining, Memento, The Wolfman (2009), and Encounter. 
9. The Virgin Suicides

Death of a Salesman 

The Great Gatsby (surprised myself when it came to mind and its society was modern for its time)

Monster

Three Billboards Outside Ebling Missouri

Pain Killers 

The Wild and Wonderful Whites of West Virginia

Manchester by the Sea 




## 3. Movie where you slowly realize the main character is the bad guy

**Score:** 51
**Body:** My partner is looking for movies where the main character is the antagonist. Specifically they’d like movies where it’s not obvious at first but throughout the film you slowly realize they aren’t the good guy

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Prestige 

The funny thing is this can work on either main character 
2. The Usual Suspects 
3. No Way Out
4. Memento
5. Starship Troopers 
6. Young Adult
7. I think it's clear from the get-go that the eponymous Mr. Ripley isn't good people, but he is one of the few villainous protagonists.
8. Fight Club 
9. Falling Down
10. Identity (2003)

## 4. I want a movie with lots of sexual tension but also with an amazing plot

**Score:** 27
**Body:**  I want a movie where even without the sex and all, the movie would still be fun to watch. I’ve noticed that some movies with insane sexual tension tend to have no real plot ( or maybe the ones that I’ve watched )

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. In the mood for love. Its great
2. Secretary (2002)
3. Portrait of a Lady on Fire
4. Body Heat (1981). 
5. Unfaithful 
6. “The Thomas Crown Affair” - the version with Pierce Brosnan.
7. sex, lies, & videotape
8. Indecent Proposal 

& Basic instinct 
9. Mr and Mrs Smith
10. Gone girl

## 5. I Miss Parody Movies, let's Make A List:

**Score:** 19
**Body:** I am looking for movies like the Scary Movie(s), Superhero Movie (2008) just pure fun and mocking, MOCKBUSTERS as they term it, sometimes the jokes were really cleaver and contextual, and sometimes they don't make sense and that's fun too.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Galaxy Quest is clearly a parody movie but with a different tone.
  - It's that rare movie that's simultaneously a great parody and a great film on its own!
2. You want the filmographies of Monty Python, Mel Brooks, ZAZ (Zucker, Abraham, Zucker) and the Wayans Brothers. Just gonna ignore Friedberg and Seltzer.


Monty Python:    
And Now for Something Completely Different (1971) (a compilation of early sketches)    
The Holy Grail (1975)    
Life of Brian (1979)    
The Meaning of Life (1983)

Wayans:   
I'm Gonna Get You Sucka (1988)

Scary Movie (2000)     
Scary Movie 2 (2001)     
White Chicks (2004)     
Little Man (2006)     
Dance Flick (2009)     
A Haunted House (2013)     
A Haunted House 2 (2014)     

Edgar Wright's Cornetto Trilogy:    
Shaun of the Dead (2004)    
Hot Fuzz (2007)   
The World's End (2013)   



Others:    
Murder by Death (1976)   
Dead Men Don't Wear Plaid (1982)   
Johnny Dangerously (1984)    
Clue (1985)   
Amazon Women on the Moon (1987)    
Meet the Feebles (1989)   
UHF (1989)    
Repossessed (1990)   
Oscar (1991)   
National Lampoon's Loaded Weapon 1 (1993)   
Fatal Instinct (1993)   
CB4 (1993)    
Silence of the Hams (1994)   
Spy Hard (1996)    
High School High (1996)   
Austin Power Trilogy (1997, 1999, 2002)    
Wrongfully Accused (1998)   
Not Another Teen Movie (2001)     
Undercover Brother (2002)    
Kung Pow: Enter the Fist (2002)     
Johnny English (2003)  & Johnny English Reborn (2011)   
Team America: World Police (2004)     
Anchorman (2004)   
Dodgeball (2004)   
Talledega Nights (2006)   
Walk Hard: the Dewey Cox Story (2007)    
Tropic Thunder (2008)   
Black Dynamite (2009)   
MacGruber (2010)    
This is the End (2013)   
A Million Ways to Die in the West (2014)    
The Ridiculous 6 (2015)

Mockumentaries   
Take the Money and Run (1969)   
The Rutles (1978)   
Zelig (1983)   
This is Spinal Tap (1984)    
Bob Roberts (1992)   
Feat of a Black Hat (1993)   
Waiting for Guffman (1996)    
Drop Dead Gorgeous (1999)   
Best in Show (2000)   
A Mighty Wind (2003)   
It's All Gone, Pete Tong (2004)   
Farce of the Penguins (2006)   
Kenny (2006)   
Borat (2006)   
Bruno (2009)   
What We Do in the Shadows (2014)
  - That scene when Moses drops one of the tablets is one of the funniest ever. (History of the World)
  - You left out one of THE best ones, UHF.

Otherwise, good list.
3. Don’t be a Menace to South Central While Drinking Your Juice in the Hood. 

Amazon Women on the Moon – slightly different. Presented like you are watching a late night movie on tv with commercials and all. I love it. 

Fear of a Black Hat

CB 4

Kentucky Fried Movie.
4. Hot Shots (1991) 

Vampires Suck (2010) 

Not Another Teen Movie (2001) 


5. Murder by Death is a parody of whodunits. It’s fun and funny regardless, but if you know the tropes and the characters (Hercule Poirot, Charlie Chan, etc) it’s even better.
6. Thats not what a mockbuster is.
7. Mystery Men is an excellent superhero satire that does not follow a scary movie type formula, but it also was way ahead of its time.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Dredd (2012) - imo this movie is criminally underrated and Karl Urban killed it as Dredd.

**Score:** 2990

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think most people that saw Dredd loved it but it wasn’t properly marketed. If I’m not mistaken there was a change in leadership at the studio that didn’t want to put any ad money into Dredd since it was produced by the previous regime which is what led to it being super under the radar 
  - My biggest turn off from watching it at all for a few years was it was heavily marketed as a 3D movie. When I finally watched it on a plane. I was blown away. I have recommended it to many people and they all loved it.
    - I think you’re right in that the 3D gimmick undercut just how great the movie was.  That said, it was the best use of 3D in that short-lived era.  The slo-mo shots were utterly mesmerizing in theatre with full effect.
2. There's a political site I like that has the greatest review of Dredd I've ever read.  Short version:  He notes how it subverts all the usual tropes like "Let me be a really poor mentor to this new person" and instead actually does his best to explain the how and why of situations.
  - That is true. Throughout the entire movie, no matter what horrible situation popped up, he was quizzing her on procedures and tactics. I always felt like even though this was a BAD situation that seemed hopeless, Dredd was chill and in control. Just made him even more of a bad ass judge.
    - And it was that kind of detail of Dredd - that Anderson even notes when she's asked to probe him during her intitial evaluation - that made him a legend among his fellow Judges; Lexx even bumps the bounty higher once his corrupt group meets with Ma-Ma, as he knows who he's going up against.
3. This movie was a gem.  I loved it and cannot believe they never made a sequel. Karl Urban is Dredd
  - ![gif](giphy|10MlVunnRFnQ2Y)


  - I said… hotshot! 
4. Truest to the comic by a mile 

## 2. Hugo Weaving in V for Vendetta is one of the best performances where an actor doesn't show his face and still crushed it.

**Score:** 1839
**Body:** Hugo is a fantastic actor but what he does in V for Vendetta is on a whole other level.

And the fact that he replaced James Purefoy six weeks into production is insane!

His line delivery and voice is so perfect, you feel every emotion just from him speaking and the mask never hinders his performance.

One of the best film performances where the actor never shows their face and still knocks it out of the park.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The voice inflection and head and body movements made up for lack of expression.  He was amazing in V for Vendetta.  One of my favorite movies.  
  - You hit the nail on the head. Every gesture and movement felt so in character.

We didn’t even need to see his face. The voice alone carried his performance. 
2. I’ve been trying to learn his opening monologue for years.
  - >*Voilà!* In view, a humble vaudevillian veteran, cast vicariously as both victim and villain by the vicissitudes of Fate. This visage, no mere veneer of vanity, is a vestige of the *vox populi*, now vacant, vanished. However, this valorous visitation of a bygone vexation stands vivified, and has vowed to vanquish these venal and virulent vermin vanguarding vice and vouchsafing the violently vicious and voracious violation of volition! *(carves the letter V in sign with his knife)* The only verdict is vengeance; a vendetta held as a votive, not in vain, for the value and veracity of such shall one day vindicate the vigilant and the virtuous. *(laughs wheezily)* Verily, this vichyssoise of verbiage veers most verbose, so let me simply add that it's my very good honour to meet you and you may call me V.
    - Are you like a crazy person?
      - He’s going to impress this girl at work when he nails it you just watch
    - When the movie first came out, I memorized this whole thing so I could recite it while I was dressed as him for that upcoming Halloween.


I nailed it the first several times, but the liquor slowly curtailed my memory as the partying went on 
3. Absolutely agree.

Edward Norton is another contender for great masked performance.

![gif](giphy|3DfDVbmubDcjsUkOoI)
  - 
“When I was sixteen, I won a great victory. I felt in that moment I would live to be a hundred. Now I know I shall not see thirty. None of us know our end, really, or what hand will guide us there. A king may move a man, a father may claim a son, but that man can also move himself, and only then does that man truly begin his own game. Remember that howsoever you are played or by whom, your soul is in your keeping alone, even though those who presume to play you be kings or men of power. When you stand before God, you cannot say, "But I was told by others to do thus," or that virtue was not convenient at the time. This will not suffice. Remember that.”


- King Baldwin IV
4. ![gif](giphy|CbYJLnm37JMre)

## 3. Actor who got popular during their teens/childhood and managed to maintain that popularity in their adulthood?

**Score:** 1225
**Body:** I think Natalie Portman would be a good answer (Leon the prof of course)

What’s your pick? 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Dicaprio 
2. Jennifer Connelly

![gif](giphy|3oEjHM3nCiieGTG5Dq|downsized)

4. ![gif](giphy|69lXr3CqBmNaTypTjC)
5. ![gif](giphy|OYzMbsyjN0pa)

Why has she not been mentioned yet?!
6. Kirsten Dunst, ever since Claudia.
7. ![gif](giphy|BNYYgeBMTXRm0)

Scarjo
8. Elijah Wood
9. Jodie foster should be the top

## 4. Which movie has such a crazy implausible plot that it makes you not care about the outcome?

**Score:** 1098
**Body:** MI: The final reckoning was insanely stupid. Who writes this crap?

* Bomb our own US city while we bomb other cities so we don't start a war
* Capture an AI in a super USB drive like a mousetrap
* Killing the AI will kill "cyberspace"
* Bombs that you can "partially disable" so that the reduced blast only kills you, not everyone else

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. the one where they drive a ship made of unobtanium to the earth's core and use nuclear weapons to "jump start" the earth.
  - The Core

So dumb but I loved it as a kid
    - The Core paved the way for the Scorcher franchise.
2. The movie is called mission impossible, do you want the missions to be reasonable?????
  - Mission completely-doable-and-scientifically-grounded. Rolls right off the tongue. 
    - That sounds like something Simon peg would say in the movie. 
3. Every Fast and Furious Movie made after Tokyo Drift

![gif](giphy|S8TtdDH5aPD4Zm3MKW|downsized)
  - ![gif](giphy|0FjtDWrg330pixL4ID)
4. Gabriel was irrelevant to me after the first movie, but they spent more time on him than the entity. 
5. Jurassic World Dominion just killed me.  I only watched it to the end for the sheer entertainment value of “I wonder what they are gonna throw in the mix next?" 

## 5. One of the most Goated scenes and wide angle shot in Cinema history(Kingdom Of Heaven)

**Score:** 788
**Body:** Kingdom Of Heaven(2005)

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. A really underrated film (Director's cut)

I originally watched it for Orlando Bloom but I fell in love with Baldwin. I was so captivated by his character, the way he spoke and his kindness and courage that I found him to be so attractive.

I remember googling who the actor was, and then I immediately watched fight club haha. Ed Norton, what an actor.
  - According to a lot of subs I’m in, the Director’s Cut is the most recognized underrated movie ever lol
    - The theatrical cut really took out the context the film needed to make sense.
2. I bought that 4K steelbook with that mask design. It's beautiful 
3. ASSEMBLE THE ARMY

GOD WILLS IT
  - Jerusalem has come.
    - I pray you retire unharmed to Damascus

![gif](giphy|3DfDVbmubDcjsUkOoI)
      - We have terms. I will send you my physicians.
4. Where can one watch the Director’s Cut in 4k?
5. Jerusalem has come….

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. The Saragossa Manuscript: Mind blowing!

**Score:** 38
**Body:** Just spent the past few hours immersed in this fascinating movie. There is so much going on in this movie that it's hard to know where to start. If I were to try and scratch the surface, the movie deals with stories nestled within stories nestled further into other stories. it's also a movie with two very distinct halves in which something of a genre switch happens after the halfway intermission. While I think it is probably impossible for anyone to fully understand its labyrinthine plot on an initial viewing, I am curious to hear from those who have seen the movie either once or many times. What are some of your key takeaways from this movie? For me, there were a lot of allusions to conflict between reality and fantasy, authorship and the importance of story telling.

 I didnt so much have a problem keeping up with whose story I was in at any given time, but I did get the sense that certain characters from one story reappeared in other stories. The most noticeable characters were Inez, and Alfonso's father. I am curious to know of any other characters or plot beats that move from one story to the next. What are some of the subtle ways that these stories within stories and characters within those stories are connected? How did you interpret the ending?

For those who haven't seen this movie, The Saragossa Manuacript is the type of movie that may appeal to fans of David Lynch and Peter Greenaway and those who enjoy movies that play with perspective in a way similar to Celine and Julie Go Boating and Rashomon.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I've been eying this film for a few years, and I finally got a chance to see it in a theater a few months ago, and man, it surprised me in so many ways. I think the most surprising thing was the avant-garde synthesizer music. In a 1965 movie shot in communist Poland? I did not expect that.
  - fun fact, the music is by Krzysztof Penderecki, best known for the piece "Threnody for the Victims of Hiroshima", which you might recognize from Twin Peaks: The Return, Episode 8. his stuff gets used a lot when auteurs want to be spooky (Lynch, Scorsese, Kubrick, Cuaron)
2. I saw this two years ago during a WJH retrospective series, then read the book, then saw it again in theater last year. I have to say, the screenwriter did an INCREDIBLE job condensing down the book, which has even more sprawling narratives and goes on another 150+ pages past where the movie (wisely) ends. 

the book does make the connections between the narratives a bit more explicit; a good amount of the characters are distant relatives or are connected in some way to the Gomelez family. it's a very dense read, but it greatly enhanced my enjoyment of the film and made the second viewing even more fun.

  - Thanks for this! I may have to check the novel. I am amazed a movie like this even exists. The fact that it is not only based on a novel, but is a faithful adaptation is awesome to know
    - you might want to also check out The Hourglass Sanatorium, another adaptation by the same director. it's much more abstract and harder to follow, but beguiling in a Tarkovsky kind of way, and the production design and cinematography (color this time) are incredible. I'd like to do the same thing of reading the book and then rewatching, because it was such a rewarding experience with Saragossa.
3. I've heard it mentioned (fondly) a few times as being in conversation with Lynch (in terms of filmmakers) and Anna Kavan (in literature) which are both comparisons that make me sit up and pay attention. But despite keeping an eye out, I've never seen it. 

A cursory glance shows me that it's streaming on Youtube, though I don't know the quality, so perhaps I'll dive into it later today and update with my initial impressions.
  - I am a big fan if Lynch and Anna Kavan's Ice. I would also draw a comparison to Borges. If you decide to check out The Saragossa Manuscript, I hope you enjoy it as much I have today. The film has two very distinct halves with an intermission around midday, but I just ploughed through. The second half is particularly exceptional.
    - I'll try to today and if not today then soon.

If you haven't read Kavan's *Eagles' Nest*, I like it just as much as *Ice*. Has the feeling of an anxiety dream come to life.
  - there's a couple of better-quality versions ripped from BluRay on the Internet Archive

## 2. Thomas Flight's video about metamodernism left me puzzled

**Score:** 20
**Body:** I recently watched a video by Thomas Flight, titled [Why Do Movies Feel So Different Now?](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5xEi8qg266g), and it left me pretty puzzled. Since I haven't studied cinema and I am just an amateur movie watcher, I am careful to say that I disagree with the vision presented in it. But I still think that it presents a really coarse vision and does not really answer the titular question.

The video claims that one of the reasons for why "they don't make them like they used to" is that current movies are metamodernist, as opposed to the modernist and post modernist movies of the past. While Flight doest state that these categories are not strict, I have the impression that his is nonetheless a coarse classification of films, bundling together all films that are not post-modernist as "modernist". I also think that this vision seems to ignore all non-american cinema, considering that people like Bergman or Rossellini definitely made non-modernist films. Maybe it's just me, but I have the suspicion that he is mixing up "classical" and "modernist".

I also don't quite understand the whole concept of meta-modernism. It says that it is a blend of modernist and post-modernist cinema, that has the self referenciality and irony of the latter but also the feelings of the former, but... I feel like a lot of post-modernist films are like that.

What do you all think?

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I think the "not being an academic" is indeed pretty central there, not as in that non-academics would be too stupid to get this, but that this modernism vs. postmodernism vs. metamodernism is an academic millieu thing to the core.


To me it makes a lot of intuitive sense, but I say that as someone who is pretty intimate with that kind of discourse and millieu.


I would thus strongly agree that this all doesnt actually capture the spirit of our era or the majority of movies being done.


I *would* say that e.g. Everything Everywhere All At Once is indeed a good example for a meta-modern film, propably self-consciously so. I would make a bet that if you asked the directors they would openly admit that they are aware of this discourse and that it propably influenced them.


Its art by and for the kind of people who even know who Frederic Jameson or David Foster Wallace even are, which are actually not so little to have real visibility in the art world, and are also often actually good artists, so they will also just capture a lot of normies who are not aware of that discourse, just because its generally good art with good jokes and good scenes, like with EEAAO.


Still, yes, its absolutely not equivalent with whats happening with movies as a whole.


>I also think that this vision seems to ignore all non-american cinema, considering that people like Bergman or Rossellini definitely made non-modernist films. Maybe it's just me, but I have the suspicion that he is mixing up "classical" and "modernist".


Yeah no, not exactly. Modernism is really not the same as classicist. Modernism would absolutely also include all kinds of avantgarde, experimental, critical, ironic works.


Postmodernism is in that discourse really a much larger thing, more about reality trickling down to the art world. For Frederic Jameson et al it really was all about society as a whole losing all sense of meaning and completely succumbing to a kind of collective nihilism and self ironic consumerism.


You could say that in the modern era experimental, avantgarde, ironic art was still consciously made with an ethos of real sense-making. There would have been a productive interplay between the earnest classicists and the experimental avantgarde. Postmodernism is when that sense of collective meaning is completely lost, and thus all it all decays into this kind of detached self-irony, both in larger society at large as well as in the art world reflecting that.


Thats also why even a lot of people who are very much into that kind of discourse dont take the meta-modernism thing terribly seriously. Its kind of like "yeah, I get it", but there is an understanding that the real break through would have to happen collectively as a real movement in society. Without that a bunch of niche artists making a certain kind of art is still very irrelevant.


You can also say that we are already moving out of the post-modern era very much, its just not this nice movement of "earnestness". No, its Trump, global nationalist movements, looming WW3, doom and gloom. Absolutely terrible, but for sure a clear break from the postmodern zeitgeist of collective bored hedonism and self-ironic consumerism.
  - >I *would* say that e.g. Everything Everywhere All At Once is indeed a good example for a meta-modern film, propably self-consciously so. I would make a bet that if you asked the directors they would openly admit that they are aware of this discourse and that it propably influenced them.

*In discussing EEAAO’s intertextuality, Kristy Puchko, author of the aforementioned article, employs the term “poignant postmodernism”; but then Kwan instead identifies “metamodernism” as a more apt description of the controlling epistemic sensibility of his film:*

*Puchko:*

*With allusions, there’s a danger that the filmmaker is using a cheap trick to engage their audience, referencing a popular thing the viewer already presumably loves. However, the allusions that Daniels employ — be they references to their casts’ other films, a rampaging Racacoonie, or a DIY Jurassic Park theme — feel* ***personal, like a poignant form of postmodernism****. When I posed this to Daniels,* ***Kwan pondered, “I think that’s the version of post-postmodernism that we’re hunting for — that metamodernism****, if I’m going to be obnoxious.”* 
  - The way I conceive it is that modernists believe truth is obscured, and that avant-garde methods and irony are about subverting and chipping away at the fake surface to reveal the hidden truth. You see this in Freud, Dada, Surrealism, art-for-art's sake. 

Postmodernism accepts there is no obscured truth, only the performance of engagement. 

For that reason we will never leave the postmodern era. It is the abiding philosophy because it has not been ideologically superseded, and won't be intellectually. The only antidote to postmdernism is not a new form of thought but thoughtlessness - a new wave of religious or fascist totalitarianism.
2. I had a very quick glance at the video just now, and I have browsed it once before, but I would definitely agree with you that “classical” is a missing term in it, and he’s confusing things by calling High Noon modernist.

Yes, I also think that exercises of taxonomy into modernist and postmodern are very often going to be subject to differences of application of the terms by different people according to individuals’ different definitions of the terms or personal classification of named artists.

Is it a question of period? Is there postmodernism at all before the mid-late sixties (Beckett, Godard)? Certain ambitious postmodern works resemble earlier modernist works very much (I would say Blood Meridian, for example.)

And your example of Bergman and most significantly Persona. We ought to think of Bergman as one of the absolute standard bearers of modernism in cinema, if we want to consider that modernism is a useful term in describing a certain kind of cinema. Yet the sequence in the middle of Persona is indeed very postmodern, or at least this is a prime example of the way that, at points of high ambition and achievement, modernism and postmodernism seem very hard to distinguish one from another.

To bring back up the question of do we use years to periodize? Like in film, would it be useful to say that modernism emerged in say, the early fifties, and lasted till the early eighties? And then there was a 25-30 cycle of postmodernism, and that brings us up to this video’s Top Gun Maverick and Everything Everywhere All At Once?
3. metamodernism is generally a spinning clothes dryer of slammed together ideas that often make little sense and are often contradictory. They tend to settle on trying to continue modernism, as may have said when they use phrases like "reinvigorating modernism" or "working with neomodernism." It's really a nostalgia for a mostly dead modernist past. 
  - The way in which these people interpret postmodern art seems very shallow, not based on any sort of academic framework as much as a collection of vague feelings about irony and sincerity, as if these added up to a tangible one-dimensional spectrum. 
    - You are correct but I think the problems lies in the terms themselves. Modernism was a self conscious movement of artists so its tenets can be understood more clearly. Postmodernism is a post-hoc term used to understand art after WW2. In my opinion there is no meaningful difference between modernism and postmodernism. Everything attributed to the latter (pastiche, parody, mixture of high and low culture, dissonance, being meta, asymmetry, etc) can be found in the former. Postmodernism is pretty much just modernism applied to mid century consumer society. 
      - In the popular sense of the word, this is true. 

But I think of pomo as a tool. A theoretical framework employed to deconstruct how power is distributed by analyzing the media. 
  - I think, fittingly, reading Infinite Jest is a good way of seeing how the contradictions of metamodernism - as far as I understand it, which it is possible I do not - resolve.
4. I think people get really hung up by thinking of Modernism, Post-Modernism, and PostPostmodernism as eras rather than movements, zeitgeists, or, even more useful, trends. They aren’t eras, at least not in film.

The thing is, in fine art and classical music and ballet and playwriting and pulitzer winning novels and these sorts of “high art” (a concept I do not believe in) things, we only really talk about innovators. So we talk about Stravinsky and Picasso and Fitzgerald, and then we talk about Cage and Warhol and Vonnegut, then we talk about Deacon and Eliasson and DFW. But some people kept doing cubism the whole time, we just don’t talk about them. Moreover, people kept doing realistic portraits and simple compositions the whole time. People kept writing pulp novels. It’s just only the new stuff made it into museums or won awards.

So when we talk about a popular art form like film, it’s not going to look as clean as when we talk about visual art, where curators have only picked things that best represent these movements to tell a cohesive story. Instead popular reception and box office sales drive the narrative. Other art forms have eras because they are curated to have eras. The public curates film, so it just doesn’t work like that.

Despite that, most early film (pre 1960) was indeed modernist. Some people were continuing the movements of pre-film “eras” like realism and romanticism, but modernism was very in vogue and selling very well in novels, and modernism’s realistic acting style in theater was very popular, and so film by and large picked it up. Non-American cinema included; modernism is a movement originating in Europe after all, particularly France. There’s an exception specifically for nonwestern film pre WW2, which was often melodrama (romanticism), but after WW2, Japanese film was largely modernist, and Chinese cinema too, though to a lesser extent.

(A side note to acknowledge the one major non-American cinema tradition that doesn’t fit the timeline. Indian cinema had its own independent trajectory, though it roughly corresponds to being romantic before Satyajit Ray, and modernist after Satyajit Ray, roughly the end of the 50s. Satyajit Ray was deeply knowledgeable about and interested in Western film, and brought a very modernist lens to India. Indian film would continue in his modernist tradition, though with far more romantic holdover elements than the west, for most of the 20th century, with postmodern influences from the west not really breaking through until the 2000s).

However Bergman, especially by 1960, is thoroughly modernist and I’m not sure what you think modernism is if you wouldn’t classify him as such. He is willing to experiment with presentation and structure, he has a very subjective style, he’s intimately focused on his characters internal lives, he explores existentialism and alienation in the modern world at length, he wrote the work in an innovative technique. He might be the single most modernist filmmaker we have.

Rossellini I wouldn’t quite call modernist; like I said some filmmakers harkened back to realism instead. Obviously Rossellini was one of them, but he did it with a somewhat modernist lens. He worked in an experimental and no traditional way despite making traditional stories, he also focused heavily on alienation and existentialism in the modern age, he was intimately concerned with his characters’ psychology. In a lot of ways, he blended realism and modernism, which resulted in a sort of “proto-postmodernism.” He would be a huge influence on the French New Wave, which is arguably the great turning point when postmodernism came to film.

Many directors don’t fit neatly, that’s why these are movements not eras. That said, outside of India, before post-modernism, it’s 80 or 90 percent modernism, even in Europe and Japan and in experimental film. Almost all of the other 10 or 20 percent is realism, romanticism, proto-postmodernism, or some sort of mishmash of the four and their various submovements.

Post-modern film came about around 1960, and some proved quite popular, and there was a period where the majority of films were taking on bits of postmodernism because it was trendy, but blockbuster films were mostly modernist still, and there were some realist undercurrents as well.

Even earlier, Film Noir had some deeply postmodern trends: bleakness, nihilism, fragmented narratives. But it’s very modernist in other ways. It doesn’t employ much distance or irony for example. But in the 60s, after the French New Wave broke down the door, arguably the first Postmodern films, and then after that we got some very clearly postmodern films like Playtime and Once Upon a Time in the West and 2001. By the 70s and 80: it was more mainstream: Monty Python, Blazing Saddles, Taxi Driver, Apocalypse Now, Bladerunner, etc.

At the same time we had some very modernist films, like the work of Spielberg, John Cassavetes and others. And those sometimes did very well.

Realistically by the 80s and 90s, most films were taking influence liberally. Some films like Princess Bride or Truman Show were distinctly postmodern, and some like Beauty and the Beast and Terms of Endearment were exceptionally modernist. But most landed in a middle zone, taking inspiration from both movements liberally. Star Wars being a key film in the middle.

Now we are in the postpostmodern “era.” That doesn’t mean that everything is postpostmodern, most of still postmodern or modern or realist or romantic or whatever else came before. It just means that the cutting edge stuff moving culture forward is postpostmodern. Especially in the “high arts.” But it does bleed through to film. And some, particularly cutting edge films do exemplify postpostmodernism well.

Just like how modernism contained surrealism and cubism, and how postmodernism contained absurdism and pop art, postpostmodernsim contains multiple movements. The three you will see discussed the most are Metamodernism, New Sincerity, and Remodernism.

Metamodernism is basically taking from Modernism and Postmodernism. I know I mention most movies do this, but they do it haphazardly and unintentionally. Metamodernism is far more intentional about it, and rather than a modernist piece here and a postmodernist piece there, a single piece can be both. This is because of the movements association with postirony. This idea that you can enjoy something both ironically and genuinely at the same time. This has always sort of existed with camp. The Rocky Horror Picture Show fans are a perfect example. In the current era, bronies are a good example. In film EEAAO provides us with a film really utilizing this in full. Barbie would fit here. As would earlier James Gunns films like the Guardians films.

New Sincerity is similar, but goes a step further by basically removing all the irony, as a harsh reaction to its predominance in postmodern art. It has been argued that Spielberg was a huge influence here, though he was just modernist the whole time like his idol John Ford, so it certainly wasn’t intentional. He just kept making movies until the trend swung his way. Big examples are Wes Andersen movies, Parks and Rec, and the more wholesome late mumblecore movies. To continue my James Gunn example, Superman is very loudly an example of this, with discussions in the film basically about the fact.

Remodernism is more of a fine art movement, and those fine artists have worked in film a bit, and identified some films that they see as part of the movement, largely pretty obscure. It’s a return to modernism, but there’s a real focus on slow and simple storytelling with it. It takes a lot from Tarkovsky. Defining it slightly broader, Perfect Days and Patterson would fit the mold quite well.

As more cutting edge pieces like EEAAO cross over and get big, and as new sincere blockbusters like Superman find more audience, you’ll notice more of these trends and techniques popping up all over film. Most films will likely still be modernist or postmodern for a while, but they’ll have elements that mark them as being in conversation with or at least taking some inspiration from postpostmodern film. Even things like Minecraft and Disney movies and MCU films bear little traits and hallmarks of these movements at this point. A film doesn’t have to be postpostmodern to have noticeable markers of the postpostmodern “era.”

I said these weren’t eras, they were movements, but they leave a little residue on everything they touch, and we can feel their context. That’s what I think Thomas Flight is feeling all over current cinema.

## 3. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (April 05, 2026)

**Score:** 15
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. *Okay, I’ll start*

[**WEEK # 274:**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)

*"...And I only want to say one thing: That if evil men are linked with one another and therefore strong, then honest men must only do likewise. It's as simple as this.."*

I finally got to watch Sergey Bondarchuk's monumental retelling of Tolstoy's historical novel **WAR AND PEACE** (1965). At an epic 7+ hours duration, this spectacular canvas has everything: Philosophical meandering about the meaning of life and love, and the senseless irrationality of warfare. Big scenes of grand balls and the opulence of the aristocracy, wolf hunting by the upper class, and unsurpassed, large scale, chaotic battles (especially the Battle of Moscow) and destruction.

With the 10,000's of extras, massive sets, groundbreaking scale and cinematographic techniques, it must be one of the "greatest" film achievements in history. 

🍿 

**HUNGARIAN MIKLÓS JANCSÓ X 2:**

🍿 **THE ROUND UP** (1966) is an austere and brutal parable about political subjugation and cruelty. In the mid 1800's, a group of Hungarian peasants are imprisoned in an isolated fort, and the guards try to ferret out who among them still support the defeated rebel Sándor Rózsa. They trick them to betray each other, but no matter what the poor bastards hope, their destiny is sealed. [The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9Edloa6Ds84) 

The top review on Letterboxd said: *"Watching this movie about an Austrian POW camp, I realized that Schindler's List is a terrible Holocaust film."*

The stark black & white cinematography, the empty Dog-forsaken steppes, the oppressive camera movement, were faithfully recreated 30 years later by Marcell Iványi in his bleak masterpiece *Wind (Szél).*

About [Jancsó's usage of the long shot](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=smmcs5HW2GI). 

🍿 **THE RED AND THE WHITE** (1967) was another harrowing war drama, similarly brutal, senseless - and beautiful. It sees the battles between the Bolsheviks and the White Army of 1917 as a cruel 'Hunger Games' where both sides kill their enemies and supporters without any regards or reservations. The upper hand changes from scene to scene, and the remorseless execution of everybody on the screen is equally pointless. There are no good guys here; they are all exactly the same. The bare landscapes near the river and the fields look magnificent in black & white and in the director's minimalist style. It's amazing that this cold, nihilistic anti-war parable was allowed to be produced at the height of Soviet power. **9/10.** 

🍿 

**MOONSTRUCK** (1987) - First watch and I never imagined that I would love it as much as I did. But I fell in love with Cher, and there's nothing I can do about it. Extremely romantic movie with stereotypical Italian everything but it works. It's joyful and funny and magical and it plays to Puccini's La Bohème. 

🍿 

**MARIANO COHN AND GASTÓN DUPRAT X 2:**

🍿 **THE DISTINGUISHED CITIZEN** (2016), my third insightful black-comedy by this Argentinian filmmaker couple. It's a prestige piece about a successful Nobel-prize novelist, who accepts an invitation from the tiny, provincial town where he was born, and which he left 40 years earlier and never visited since. He's been living in Europe, became an elitist and world-wide literary celebrity, but all his books are based on characters from the mythical "Macondo" of his childhood. A fantastic premise, very well made. The ingenious plot twists are like the stories he writes, and his uncomfortable adventures in this sleepy, forgotten place are hilarious.   

[The trailer](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=2f5La9q2_k8). 💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes. **The most unexpected delight of the week.**

🍿 Their Spanish 2022 absurdist comedy **OFFICIAL COMPETITION** is a terrific three-hander. An 80-yo billionaire decides to finance a film that will cement his legacy. He hires reclusive director Penélope Cruz, an eccentric female David Lynch-type, and she brings in celebrity star Antonio Banderas, and 'Distinguished citizen' 'method actor' Oscar Martínez, who challenge each other in an absurdist battle of egos. It's a meta-parody about film-acting, played in super-modernist, large, empty spaces, and is funny as hell. 8+/10. Recommended. Re-watch ♻️.

🍿 

**CARLOS HUGO CHRISTENSEN X 2:**

🍿 *"It's the eternal struggle between the child and the monster.."*

[Cornell Woolrich](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Cornell_Woolrich) was a tragic crime writer, whose pulp novels were the sources for many movies, including Hitchcock's 'Rear Window' and Truffaut's 'The Bride Wore Black' and 'Mississippi Mermaid'. The disturbing Argentinian Noir **IF I SHOULD DIE BEFORE I WAKE** (1952) was another. It's the story of a misbehaving school boy, the son of a 2nd rank police inspector, who discovers a child murderer lurking in his neighborhood. [It's as suspenseful and dark as 'The night of the hunter'.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Z2G_eLy-uBs)

It checks off a good one from two of my lists: That of [Foreign Language Noir](https://letterboxd.com/sakana1/list/winter-of-foreign-language-noir/by/rating/) as well as [Argentinian Noir from the 40's and 50's.](https://letterboxd.com/lmgiannone/list/argentinian-noir/by/rating/) 

🍿 Carlos Hugo Christensen was one of the ["iconic figures of the Classical Era of Argentinian Cinema".](https://letterboxd.com/lmgiannone/list/argentinian-noir/by/rating/)

His 1967 **THE BOY AND THE WIND** was the first Brazilian LGBT films ever made. A 25-yo handsome engineer is standing trial in a small town, accused of the disappearance of a teenager, and implicitly of having a sexual relationship with him. He refuses a legal defense, and in his speech before the court, he describes the bond between them. It so happened that the boy was a "Wind whisperer", who would summon the powers of nature at will, and who "went back" as they were together on his last day in town. The irrational connection to nature and magic is an allegory for same-sex love. 

🍿 

**IT WAS JUST AN ACCIDENT** (2025), my 10th gripping drama by Jafar Panahi, and his latest award-winning political "thriller". A traumatized motley group of people who were imprisoned by the Iranian regime, manages to capture a man who may or may not be the one who had sadistically tortured them, and decide to murder him. However, they are not 100% sure if it is really him. It's a moral quandary that is disclosed slowly, and played out brilliantly. Based on Panahi's own two incarcerations. The final moment of catharsis puts it all in perspective. [The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=nF04v-ze2Yc)

It was made by the amazing French production company MK2.

*(More below)*
  - *(Part 2)*

An existential Greek Noir comedy from 1956 in black & white and with score by Manos Hatzidakis? Yes, please...

**THE OGRE OF ATHENS** tells of an unassuming middle-age bank teller, who resembles a police sketch of a wanted notorious criminal. The plot twist: As this meek and lonely non-entity finds himself on the run from the law he starts embracing his new character, until the mistaken role-playing comes to a tragic end. Even though it's an important work in modern Greek Cinema, it's not objectively very good. I can see however how a new re-make - maybe by a Yorgos Lanthimos - could elevate it into something much more interesting. 

🍿 

*“It’s complicated…”*

**IS THIS THING ON?** (2026) is the 3rd romantic comedy directed by Bradley Cooper, so very different from his 'A star is born' and 'Maestro'. A "comedy of remarriage" reminiscent of 'Marriage Story' with gorgeous Laura Dern playing the wife instead of the lawyer. It tries to walk a fine line between the sad dissolution of a 26 year relationship, and the midlife crisis that pushes sad sack Will Arnett to use stand-up as a form of therapy. It's modern, melancholic and sober. The first half is full of cringe standup situations, but then comes *"That Scene"* right in mid-film which is so powerful, it makes you vested from that point on in the divorced couple and wish they can get back together again. 6/10. 

🍿 

My 3rd re-watch ♻️ of the tight thriller **EMILY THE CRIMINAL** (2022). Uncomfortably tense, it feels like an "American Nightmare" documentary about the working poor, the modern gig economy and the job market with no mercy and no prospects. Poverty and helplessness, exploitation and feelings of circling down the drain which the bottom 50% (or more?) of all workers must live through. 

Also including a dangerous hookup with Youcef, a realistically-depicted Lebanese hustler. 

Now go ahead and make a sequel, but this time make the protagonist an ordinary-looking anti-hero loser, not a hot, young Aubrey Plaza in a tight camisole. See how that lands!

🍿 

*“🎶 Sit on my face and tell me that you love me 🎶”*

**A LIAR'S AUTOBIOGRAPHY** (2012), a middling not too funny, not too revealing animation of Graham Chapman's fictionalized bio which he completed and read-aloud just before his death of cancer at 48. It uses too many cheap animation styles (14) and loose structure without a center. Cameron Diaz voiced Sigmund Freud. No juice. Interesting mostly for Python completists. 

🍿 

**THE SHORTS:**

🍿 I’ve seen some Iranian titles that were produced by the “Institute for the Intellectual Development of Children and Young Adults” (“Kānoon”). This was a prominent cultural organisation that was at the forefront of the liberal, pre-revolutionary Iran of the 60’s and 70’s. Through the production of many books, music, theatre works and more than 60 movies, it held a central place in the hearts of a whole generation of kids. It also helped launch the careers of many famous filmmakers of that era, among them Abbas Kiarostami, whose early films were about and for children. His **THE COLOURS** (1976. 17 min.) was characteristic of these cheerful educational shorts, similar in tone to the early ‘Sesame Street’ / ‘Muppet Show’ output. “*Baby’s first Kiarostami film*”…

It's wild how darker Iran looked in the media after 1979, when the secular, materialistic society was transformed into a repressed theocracy. But here, everything looked vibrant and happy.

🍿 **IN A QUIET ROOM** (2024. 12 min.). An elderly woman goes about her morning, alone, working in her garden, having a visit from her son. A heart-felt gen. 8/10. 

🍿 **TANGHI ARGENTINI** (2006, 14 min.), a Belgian Christmas short about a middle aged office guy who wants to learn how to dance the Tango in two weeks.

🍿 **EL CARRETERO** (2014, 5 min.), sung by the workers walking back after a long day at the fields. From ‘Buena Vista Social Club’. *[Female Director.]*

🍿 

[**(ALL MY FILM REVIEWS - HERE)**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)
2. **Bad Faith** (2024) \*\*\* A documentary about the history of Christian Nationalism that is blatantly designed to condemn and terrify ahead of the 2024 election. It connects a vast conspiracy-like network of well-funded organizations working with churches to overtake American politics and digs into the roots of the movement, and while thorough and informative, the delivery gets a bit monotonous. 

**All the Way** (2016) \*\*\*\* A poorly-titled historical drama about Lyndon B. Johnson's campaign for the presidency following Kennedy's assassination. The first half seems modeled after Spielberg's *Lincoln* as Johnson schemes to pass a desegregation bill, which causes him to lose the Southern vote of his own party. The second half is muddled and less urgent since we already know he's going to win, but it's all done with great period detail that doesn't call attention to itself. Bryan Cranston gives a transformative performance and practically disappears behind convincing makeup.

**Toto the Hero** (1991) \*\*\*\* A man spends his entire life in rivalry with his neighbor, with whom he believes he was switched at birth. This is a quirky and sometimes dark story that's convoluted and jumps all over in time and in and out of fantasy yet remains coherent and easy to follow. Perhaps it gets a little too cute and wrapped up in its own contrivances, but fans of *Amelie* should check this out. 

**Ulzana's Raid** (1972) \*\*\*\* Burt Lancaster leads a group of soldiers on the hunt for a warring band of Apaches led by Ulzana. The *Little House on the Prairie* aesthetic takes a surprisingly brutal turn in the first act (although not too surprising when you realize it's a Robert Aldrich movie). After that the film is a meditation on racism and brutality in the west, which Lancaster understands all too well but he can't get it through the white man's thick skull. He's countered by a quixotic Bruce Davison, who struggles to understand the Apache way's against his own Christian morality. Most of the movie, however, is a clever cat-and-mouse chase as Lancaster tries to outwit Ulzana. 

**Jeopardy** (1953) \*\*\* A B-movie made watchable by an A-level cast who can deliver the corny dialogue with a straight face. Barbara Stanwyck was apparently coming back from semi-retirement and at least picked a film where she got to do most of the action. The initial premise apparently didn't have enough jeopardy, so they threw in Ralph Meeker as a ruthless killer. He makes the most of it, and it's very similar to The Hitch-Hiker, which was released about the same time. John Sturges' action directing is clear and realistic, but he doesn't bring much character nuance. At 70 minutes, it feels more like a good TV movie. 

# SHORT FILMS

**Jackals and Fireflies** (2023) \*\*\* Charlie Kaufman's portrait of New York City as told by the poetic muse Eva H.D. A nice little short filled with odd observations in a search for place and identity, but hardly essential for Kaufman fans.

**How to Shoot a Ghost** (2025) \*\*\* Another Charlie Kaufman short that's very similar to *Jackals and Fireflies*, but now the people are ghosts adding history and immigration to the quest for identity and connection. I don't recommend watching the two shorts together.

**Gap-Toothed Women** (1987) \*\*\*  Oddly disturbing documentary about women with gaps in their teeth (why not men too?) Pointing out the issue just makes it so prominent that you want to look away, esp. when there's a closeup. While the point is to question beauty standards, it's more about how these women just get on with their lives.

**The Fixer Uppers** (1935) \*\*\*\* Laurel and Hardy are door-to-door greeting card salesmen that get tangled up trying to fix a loveless marriage and end up being challenged to a duel. The convoluted story has some good plot twists. Teri Garr look-alike Mae Busch plays the wife.

**Cops** (1922) \*\*\*\* While I've seen parts of this film plenty of times, I'm not sure I've seen the whole thing. The first half was new to me. This is an ambitious short with a cast of hundreds and several memorable, meme-ready gags. 

**A Dog's Life** (1918) \*\*\*\* I've seen this Chaplin short a few times, but I'm going through a long list of his shorts and it wasn't checked off yet so I gave it another watch. It's basically *The Kid*, but with a cute dog instead of a kid, and it's one of his more famous ones. 
3. **What Dreams May Come (1998, Vincent Ward)**

I like Robin Williams and he was a good choice for this movie, but I didn’t care for the movie itself. It felt overly sappy to me if not boring at times. May have found the special effects (the painting) more impressive when it first came out in the 90’s but not today. Just wasn’t for me. **5/10**

## 4. Is there a deeper connection between Holy Motors and The Lovers on the Bridge?

**Score:** 5
**Body:** I noticed a few things in the scene with Kylie Minogue:

1. It takes place at almost the exact same location: Pont Neuf
2. They haven't seen each other in 20 years: There is 20 years time between both movies
3. It looks like before she jumps she looks at the bench where most of Lovers on the Bridge took place

I am less sure of this, but I believe that scene more than any other is about his late partner Yekaterina Golubeva, who died about 20 years after Lovers on the Bridge came out. The movie is dedicated to her (it's in the credits).

### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 5. Die My Love -- Does it Work?

**Score:** 2
**Body:** I recently watched Die My Love, and had quite a few conflicting feelings about it:

Jennifer Lawrence should definitely play more roles like this. I feel there is a darkness in her that is a bit untapped. People have tried like that weird Russian spy movie she did, but Lynne Ramsay definitely understands the tone that needs to be pulled out of her as an actor. I think they really vibrate on the same frequency, especially now with Lawrence being a bit older and a mother, etc. I think they work great together and should work together again, but this adaptation wasn't the best fit. I almost don't get why it had to be an adaptation at all -- it seemed like the source material really held this back from being something better or more memorable. It's funny because of how severe many of the scenes are...you would think things would be burned into your brain. But something just felt hard to grab onto. Maybe that effect is the point (what with the mental health aspect of the film) but I have a hard time believing that.

Looing back, I feel like you can also see some of this in the way it was marketed -- everything from the clips they chose to share to the font on the trailers (lol) -- seemed to suggest they had little idea what to do with this movie once it was finished. It's surprisingly (IMO) not arty or dangerous enough to be an arthouse breakout and obviously not mainstream enough to be an Oscar-type movie. I don't think it was trying to be "iconic" but it's not that either. It just landed in this weird middleground. It's defintiely a prime example of the parts being greater than the whole. So many memorable scenes and little moments, but it really did not come together for me.

 I also think Pattinson was pretty miscast. I think he did ok, but I didn't get who the character was really supposed to be, nor did it seem intentionally obfuscated. The movie made me wonder if he has enough range as an actor to pull off what I think it demanded. IMO would have been just as good if not better to cast an unknown theater actor, or someone who reads as a bit more nerdy etc. I had trouble understanding how much power he really had over her, if he was supposed to be a f\*\*\*\*boy or a nerd, it just wasn't clear. I didn't believe it, nor did I feel challenged by the presentation of it. I also think a lesser known actor could have allowed Lawrence to shine more.

  
No idea why Lakeith Stanfield was in this. They could have saved some money and cast an unknown. But, it goes to show that something about this movie does feel very packaged in a weird way.

  
All the night scenes kind of threw me off with the extreme blue filter. I wasn't sure if it was supposed to be a passable Day-for-Night, or if it was just an artistic decision that didn't really need to look like night. I came up with the theory that it just represented a subconscious realm, and that seemed to work. Because no way the filmmakers thought it actually looked like night.

  
The editing was tight. Already, the story is kind of shapeless and hard to engage with, so the editing really succeeded in helping bring us in as a viewer. It felt like we endured a lot, and that we were trapped with this couple, but it also propelled every sequence forward and sort of flowed with the way Lawrence's character thought/felt.

  
Of course the cinematography is phenomenal. Like, absolutely phenomenal and so indulgent without ever being unnecessary. It really added dimension, and nothing was wasted. I haven't seen a movie that risks so much to look so defined in a while. I was in awe at the cinematography. Everything seemed to fly out of the screen and it imbued the movie with this scary feeling of violence, dread, etc. There's one shot of, like, five mirrors when she is brushing Sissy Spacek's hair, and it's pretty gnarly. There are also amazing POV shots of the car driving on the road that make you feel like you're about to crash. I almost want to rewatch it with the sound off.

  
Some beautiful sequences -- I loved the wedding dance, when she took off her clothes to get in the pool, the fire, the mental hospital stretch, her dancing around the bridal suite while that guy plays the guitar, watching her interact with other neighbors, the incredible sound design. Some really good stuff.

  
TL;DR: the movie doesn't work as a whole but has some amazing elements. Jennifer Lawrence needs to do keep doing more arty, sparse, weird shit.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I hated it. It’s just Jennifer Lawrence doing the same thing over and over for two hours. It’s very static with no arc. There was no understanding of her character or what the movie was about. Like her character is apparently a writer, but you could take that out and it wouldn’t have changed the movie at all because she wasn’t playing that. 

Pattinson also has become one of the most annoying actors to watch for me because all he does is put on a different accent for every role and nothing else. 

The relationship between them is completely undefined so it’s hard to feel much of anything for them. 

I feel like Hollywood doesn’t understand the difference between famous actors and talented ones because this movie really relies on the actors seeing what isn’t said in the script and building a behavioral logic through the acting to bring that out. That’s never been the strong suit for either of these actors. 

---
